content.assessment: Delaying selection to age 14 addresses the fiscal-incidence problem directly by removing the two earliest funnel stages where SES-linked access advantages are most pronounced (F-live-education_finance-03), and would in principle equalize the per-pupil funding pool by eliminating the segregated cost structure implied by tracks serving unequal populations. However, the corpus lacks proposal-level transition cost estimates, so the fiscal magnitude of restructuring school networks, retraining or reallocating staff, and managing a shrinking set of relevant institutions (F-live-education_finance-01) cannot be quantified here. The value-for-money case rests on inequality-reduction evidence that is itself contested for its effect on mean performance (F-live-education_finance-02).
opportunities: Consolidation could reduce administrative duplication costs currently spread across three entry-point systems (F-live-education_finance-01)
strengths: Directly targets the entry points most associated with SES-based access inequity, per value-added divergence beginning early in the pipeline (F-live-education_finance-03)
Potentially removes the fiscal rationale for parallel elite/non-elite funding parity mismatches by consolidating the population served under one entry point (F-live-education_finance-06)
threats: Absent quantified transition costs, the reform risks unbudgeted implementation expense, particularly if it mirrors disruption patterns noted in comparable systems
Elite/private-track incentives could migrate into non-public preparation markets, reproducing the SES-driven access gap in a new form (F-live-education_finance-06)
weaknesses: No cost estimate available in the corpus for transition, staff reallocation, or capital reallocation across a restructured school network
Mean-performance effects of delayed tracking are statistically inconsistent, undermining a clean value-for-money justification (F-live-education_finance-02)
